subject: Cross-Curricular — Project Launch
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Day 1: Meet the Project

lesson_overview.overview: Day 1 is a shared whole-class launch. Every student — regardless of subject enrollment — experiences the same opening: they meet the client, understand the real job, see the space, and commit to a role. All students have K designations. Oral response is full evidence. Teacher reads aloud before anyone writes. The second half of the session splits into subject-streamed tasks, but the debrief brings everyone back together. The goal today is engagement, not content mastery.
lesson_overview.essential_question: What does our team need to do — and why does it matter?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Every real project starts with someone who needs something. Our job is to understand what they need, plan it carefully, and make it happen.

session_arc.description: Visual agenda on board. Teacher names what is happening today in plain language.
Teacher shows photos or takes class to the actual site. Students draw or describe what they see.
Teacher reads the client brief aloud twice. Students mark key words on their sheet.
Class discusses: what do we need to plan? build? grow? record? Teacher scribes on board.
Students work on a short subject-specific first look. English: vocabulary and questions. Math: site sketch and first estimates.
Each student or pair shares one thing. Teacher records responses on the class anchor chart.
Each student completes or dictates their project commitment card.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Today is about belonging to the project. Every student needs to leave knowing: what we are doing, why it matters, and that they have a real role in it. This session does not assess — it invites.
learning_goals: Every student can say (in any form) what this project is and who the client is.
Every student has identified one thing they will contribute.
English-stream students have met the project vocabulary and the client brief.
Math-stream students have made a first estimate of the space and identified what they will measure.
k_designation_notes: Read the client brief aloud twice before students see it on paper. First read: they just listen. Second read: they follow along.
The site visit or site photos are essential — many students need the physical reality before the task makes sense.
Accept all forms of response today: oral, drawn, pointed to, scribed by teacher or EA, or tapped out.
Spotty attendance means today's commitment card is a re-entry tool too — any student who missed Day 1 can complete it on Day 2.
Do not rush the settle phase. Students with K designations often need longer to regulate before they can engage.
materials: Site photos printed or displayed on screen (or access to the actual outdoor space)
Client brief printed one per student (large print available)
Day 1 student launch packet — one per student
Pencils, markers, and highlighters at every table
Class anchor chart paper and markers (teacher-managed)
Sentence frame strips posted visibly in room
Camera or phone for students to photograph the site (optional)

timing.activity: Visual agenda: show today's plan on the board as pictures + words. Say it aloud: 'Today we are meeting our project. We will see the space, read a letter from the client, and decide what our job is.'
Site visit or photo tour. Ask: what do you notice? what is already there? what is missing? Students sketch the space on their launch packet. Accept any marks on paper.

First read of the client brief — teacher reads aloud while students listen. No writing yet. Then say: 'I'm going to read it again. This time, circle or underline anything that tells us what they want.'
Second read — students follow on their own copy. Teacher pauses at key sentences. Class discusses: who is the client? what do they need? where will it go?
Job breakdown: teacher draws the project on the board as a simple diagram (plan → build → plant → record → report). Students help name what goes in each box. Scribe all responses.

Subject-streamed task. Students open to their subject page in the launch packet. English-stream: vocabulary and what I noticed. Math-stream: site sketch with labels and first estimates. Teacher and EA circulate.
Share: each student says or shows one thing they recorded or drew. Teacher adds it to the class anchor chart. No wrong answers.
Commitment card: each student writes, draws, or dictates one thing they will do or contribute to this project. Teacher collects or photographs.

teacher_notes: If the outdoor space is not available, use three or four good photos of the planned location — projected large.
The client brief can be the real school administrator or teacher who requested the project. A real face and name makes a significant difference for engagement.
Some students will not write a word today. That is fine. Their oral participation and their sketch count.
Post the sentence frames before students arrive: 'The client is ___.' / 'The job is ___.' / 'I am going to ___.'
The class anchor chart stays on the wall for the duration of the project — it is the class memory for students with attendance gaps.
If an EA is present, assign them to the scribe station during the streamed task phase.
teacher_guide.flex_if_running_short: If time is tight, cut the streamed task phase and do only the commitment card. The job breakdown is the essential moment — do not cut it.
teacher_guide.flex_if_running_long: If energy is high and time allows, let students take photos of the space on a phone or tablet. These become reference images for the whole project.

content.subtitle: Day 1 — Meet the project, meet the client, understand the job.

rows: 1. See the space — where our planter will go
2. Read a letter from the client
3. Figure out: what is the job?
4. Decide what we each will do

content.scenario: This is where the planter box will go. Look carefully.
Someone at this school has asked us to build something. They wrote us a letter. Listen while I read it.
prompts: Who is asking us to do this?
What do they want us to build?

rows: PLAN — choose the right design and figure out what we need
BUILD — construct the planter box safely and carefully
GROW — plant, water, and track what happens

content.scenario: When the project is done, we tell the client what we did and what happened. We show them the evidence.

rows: English 10 / 11 / 12 → open to Page 2: vocabulary and client questions
Math 8 → open to Page 3: sketch the space and estimate
Workplace Math 10 → open to Page 3: sketch the space and estimate
Use the sentence frames on the wall if you need them.
You have about 20 minutes. Ask for help anytime.

prompts: What is one thing you wrote, drew, or noticed today?
One question you have about the project:
One thing you think you can help with:
One thing I will do on this project: ___
I am most interested in: plan / build / grow / report (circle one)

worksheet.title: Day 1 Launch Packet — Plan, Build, Grow
instructions: Page 1 is for everyone. Your teacher will read the client brief aloud.
You can write, draw, circle, or ask someone to scribe for you.

tasks.label: Page 1 — For Everyone: The Site
tasks.prompt:
Look at the space where our planter box will go.

Draw what you see:

[drawing space]

Circle words that describe the space:
big   small   sunny   shady   near a wall   in the open   on concrete   on grass   indoors   outdoors

render_hints.heading: What does the space look like?
render_hints.help: You can draw, label, or just circle words. All three are great.

tasks.label: Page 1 — For Everyone: The Client Brief
tasks.prompt:
Your teacher will read the client brief aloud. As you listen, circle or underline:

→ WHO is asking us to do this?
→ WHAT do they want?
→ WHERE will it go?

After the second read, write or draw your answers:

The client is: ___________________
They want us to: ___________________
It will go: ___________________

render_hints.help: Sentence frames: The client is ___. The job is to ___ . It will go ___.

tasks.label: Page 2 — English Stream: Project Vocabulary
tasks.prompt:
These words will come up a lot in this project. Read them with your teacher.

client — the person who gave us the job
planter box — a container for growing plants
materials — what we use to build it (wood, screws, soil)
dimensions — the size: length, width, and height
report — telling the client what we did and what happened

Choose TWO words. Write or say a sentence using each one.

render_hints.heading: English — Vocabulary First Look
render_hints.help: Example: 'The client is Ms. Chen. She gave us the job.' Try your own!

tasks.label: Page 2 — English Stream: Questions for the Client
tasks.prompt:
A good team asks questions before they start. What would you want to ask the client?

My question 1: ___________________
My question 2: ___________________

Circle what matters most to you right now:
what it looks like   how big it is   how much it costs   who will use it   what we plant in it

render_hints.help: Question starters: Can you tell me... / How big should it be... / What do you want planted...

tasks.label: Page 2 — English Stream: What I Noticed
tasks.prompt:
In one or two sentences, describe what you noticed about the space today.

Sentence frame: I noticed that the space is ___. I think this means we will need to ___.

You can also draw your observation instead.

tasks.label: Page 3 — Math Stream: Sketch the Space
tasks.prompt:
Draw a simple top-down sketch of the space where the planter will go.

[drawing space — top view]

Label what you can:
→ A wall or fence (if there is one)
→ Which direction is sunny
→ How many steps wide the space looks to you

render_hints.heading: Math — First Look at the Space
render_hints.help: Top view means you are looking down from above, like a map. Just do your best estimate.

tasks.label: Page 3 — Math Stream: First Estimates
tasks.prompt:
Without measuring yet — just estimate.

I think the space is about _______ steps wide.
I think the space is about _______ steps long.
If I turned my steps into centimetres, 1 step ≈ 70 cm.

Estimated width: _______ steps × 70 cm = _______ cm
Estimated length: _______ steps × 70 cm = _______ cm

Do these seem like reasonable planter box dimensions? Y / N
Why or why not? ___________________

render_hints.help: Estimates are guesses based on what you can see. There is no wrong answer here — we will measure properly next week.

tasks.label: Page 3 — Math Stream: Budget Thinking
tasks.prompt:
Before we know exact costs, let's think about money.

Circle your best guess for the total project budget:
$50   $100   $200   $500   $1000   more than $1000

What costs the most when you build something? Circle:
wood   screws   soil   plants   tools   labour

Why did you choose that? ___________________

render_hints.help: This is a prediction — you will find out the real budget in the client brief.

tasks.label: Page 4 — For Everyone: Commitment Card
tasks.prompt:
My name: ___________________

One thing I will do on this project:
___________________

I am most interested in (circle one):
PLAN     BUILD     GROW     REPORT

One question I have:
___________________

render_hints.heading: My Project Commitment
render_hints.help: You can write, draw, dictate, or point. Every answer counts.

embedded_supports: Sentence frames posted in room: The client is ___. The job is ___. I can help by ___.
All tasks accept oral, drawn, or written response
Teacher or EA scribing available throughout
Vocabulary words remain visible on the board for the full session
Circling/pointing is a valid response for every question that includes choice words
success_criteria: I know who the client is.
I know what we are building and where it will go.
I have made one contribution to the class anchor chart.
I completed my commitment card in any format.

exit_ticket.title: Day 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt:
Tell me (say, write, draw, or point):

1. Who is the client?
2. What are we building?
3. One thing you will do on this project.
success_criteria: Student can identify the client in any form.
Student can describe the project in any form.
Student has named one contribution they will make.
exit_ticket.teacher_notes: Exit check can be done as a group check-out question at the door — student answers verbally as they leave. No written response required if the session was full. Commitment card can substitute.
